title: Quote Text Objects
subtitle: i" / a" / i' / a' — operate on a quoted string without finding its boundaries.
summary: i" is the contents of the surrounding double-quoted string. a" includes the quotes themselves. Same for ' and `.

blocks.text: Editing a string literal? Cursor anywhere inside it. {key:c}{key:i}{key:"} blanks the contents and drops you in to type the new ones. {key:d}{key:a}{key:"} removes the quotes and contents together.

blocks.label: Delete the quoted string AND the quotes

blocks.title: How Vim Finds the Quotes
blocks.text:
Quote objects use a different search heuristic from bracket objects. For a quote character `q`, Vim:

  1. Counts the q-characters from the start of the line up to the cursor.
  2. If the count is **odd**, the cursor is *inside* a quoted region: the matching pair is the most recent q-pair containing the cursor.
  3. If the count is **even**, the cursor is *outside*: the matching pair is the *next* one to the right.

This is why you can press {key:c}{key:i}{key:"} from outside a string and still hit the right one — Vim looks rightward. It also means quote objects don't span multiple lines (the count restarts each line) and they don't understand backslash-escaping inside the string.

The three quote characters are {key:"}, {key:'}, and {key:`} (backtick). All three behave identically — the object character matches the literal quote you typed. You don't need a separate object per language.
{key:i}{key:"} and {key:a}{key:"} are charwise — the cursor stays on the line. So {key:d}{key:a}{key:"} doesn't delete the line; it deletes the quoted span and leaves the rest of the line intact.
